e50000-12: The verdict is a toss-up between X and 2H, under half an IMP apart. X is the mainstream action: with 4=5=1=3 and a singleton diamond, a competitive double of a limped-in 2D is shape-perfect even at 8 HCP, and partner can choose either major at the two level or sit with fat diamonds. 2H names the five-card suit and wins the declaration, but on the card it shows a sound overcall; non-vulnerable against vulnerable, the fifth heart and the dead auction license the stretch, at the cost of partner competing again expecting more. Flexibility against directness — the deal pool cannot separate them. P is the real leak, nearly 2 IMPs: with a singleton in their suit and partner marked with a few values, never sell out to a cozy 2D. When opponents die low, shape outranks high cards.
e50004-24: A three-way toss-up: with confidence intervals above a full IMP, the deal pool cannot separate these calls. 3C runs to the eight-card fit — QJ94 opposite partner's usual four-card preference — buying the hand cheaply while jamming West's red-suit advances. 2S retreats to a suit partner has limited himself in, and it suggests a sixth card; KQJ76 plays nearly like six, which licenses the lie, but partner will act throughout believing in the extra trump. P keeps 2C doubled alive, and East, behind declarer (West is behind dummy), can convert with club length; more often the opponents escape to a red fit and the plus evaporates, so P trails a shade. Trump quality, fit size, and defensive chances each back a different call, and when they pull this evenly, the table decision is a style choice.
e50004-30: A dead-heat toss-up: barely a tenth of an IMP splits three roads. A stem note: the 2S rebid on this 5=2=2=4 hand already shaded the card, since repeating the suit often promises six. 3C is the textbook route — natural, patterning out below 3NT, and it lets partner offer notrump with the normal 12-14 balanced rebid, raise clubs, show a third heart, or drive with the rare big hand. 3NT is fast arrival with soft minors: it gains a tempo and loses only the occasional superior strain. 3H manufactures support on the doubleton AK; two top honours outplay many small three-card holdings — the case for it — but partner believes in an eight-card fit and will raise to a 5-2 game or push on that basis. When the numbers refuse to choose, honest pattern is the habit that pays.
e50005-26: With 4=1=4=4 and a singleton heart, the verdict is a toss-up between 2C and 1D. 2C bids what four trumps and a ruffing value are worth, stealing the doubler's cheap advances; the third-seat 1C may be only three cards and partner will read a chunkier raise, but that is a judgment stretch, not a card breach. 1D names the hand's real values just as cheaply and keeps every strain open. Preemption against description — the pool cannot separate them. 1S costs about three-quarters of an IMP: T862 is a poor suit to volunteer when the doubler advertises the majors, even though he is your RHO and would play in front of you in a spade contract. P costs most of all, handing the doubling side an uncontested auction. With weak responding hands, the losing call is usually silence.
e50018-10: 3C is the accepted winner, by more than two IMPs. Partner's 2D rebid shows a minimum opener with five-plus diamonds opposite this singleton: a misfit, and the misfit rule is to run to your longest suit. KJT652 opposite a likely small doubleton is a real source of tricks, the correction costs one step, and partner needs extreme shape to insist on diamonds. 2S is the costly trap, three IMPs behind: a fresh suit at the two level promises four, so partner with three-card support raises what he believes is a 4-3 fit — it is actually 3-3 — a level higher. P fails as well: it leaves partner in 2D with a singleton opposite five-plus diamonds and no outside source of tricks, minus in comfort. In a misfit, the cheapest exit is the longest suit, not the cheapest call.
e50023-18: P wins by a modest but real half-IMP over X. This is a soft, defense-facing minimum — QJ tight, QJ8, scattered jacks — worth more defending a spade partial than declaring. Pass is not surrender: partner with eight-plus values acts again over 1S, so the diamond fit is rarely lost, and when partner is broke, defending 1S quietly is the top score. X is the card-perfect support double, showing exactly the three diamonds held; its flaw is not honesty but ambition — it volunteers a flat minimum toward the two level, and it loses nearly five times as often as it gains. 2C would promise a sixth club and bury the diamond news. 1NT stakes a 12-count on a doubleton stopper. A free action in competition should show extra playing strength, not merely an accurate shape.
e50023-20: A coin-flip toss-up: half an IMP, inside the noise. 2H upgrades 7 HCP into the 9-11 the card wants, and the licence is real — a fifth trump, a prime ace, a working K9, a doubleton — so a shapely doubler carries the raise to the vulnerable game. Two costs offset it: partner holds you to the announced 9-11 and overreaches opposite his flat 14-15s, and the club K9 sits in front of the opener, finessable, so that card is part illusion. 1H is not passive, because the hand has a second move: when the doubler invites, this collection accepts, so the slow route reaches most of the same games while resting low opposite square minimums. Immediacy against recoverability — the forces cancel. Upgrade when the tricks are there — but partner bids your announced values, not your actual ones.
